title: Gulliver never stopped travelling
gist: Three centuries on, Swift's Gulliver's Travels endures as a satire whose targets (law, war, colonialism, science, immortality) remain recognisable, and whose form seeded a lineage from Verne and Wells to Orwell and Star Trek.

insights.claim: Gulliver's Travels demonstrates the longevity of ideas in art: a 1726 satire became so familiar it graduated into a children's book while retaining its critical bite for adult readers.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: Illustrates how subversive content survives by being culturally domesticated, a pattern of idea persistence.
insights.quote: by graduating to become a children's book, something so familiar that its destination is the nursery
insights.locator: In the 300 years since its publication

insights.claim: Swift pioneered using fictional remote settings to critique contemporary power safely, a device later adopted explicitly by Star Trek creator Gene Roddenberry to discuss forbidden political subjects.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: Names the displaced-setting technique as a recurring vehicle for political commentary across media.
insights.quote: maybe if I could have all my stories happen on far off planets then I could talk about those things

insights.claim: Gulliver's Travels established a lineage running through Jules Verne, H.G. Wells, and Orwell's Animal Farm, which 'lives firmly in the shadow' of Swift's work.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: Traces an intellectual lineage showing how foundational works propagate through later culture.
insights.quote: it's impossible not to see the legacy of Swift's masterpiece around us
insights.locator: Animal Farm is far from being alone

insights.claim: Swift's portrait of colonialism as piracy dressed up as a civilising mission anticipates modern critiques of imperial conquest by three centuries.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: Shows the durability of anti-imperial critique, useful for contextualising contemporary debates.
insights.quote: an harmless People, are entertained with kindness
insights.locator: there is an especially rewarding section

insights.claim: Swift's recurring abhorrence of science and progress, lampooning the Royal Society via the Grand Academy of Lagado, marks him as a counter-Enlightenment satirist of technological hubris.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: A historical antecedent of skepticism toward scientific/technological overreach.
insights.quote: Swift's recurring abhorrence for science or progress
insights.locator: The second theme is Swift's recurring abhorrence

insights.claim: Swift used the immortal Struldbruggs to puncture the fantasy of never dying, depicting endless life as misery rather than blessing.
insights.speaker: Alec Marsh
insights.so_what: A literary counterweight to modern longevity/immortality enthusiasm.
insights.locator: Luggnagg also boasts a race of immortals
